required.title: [Read Aloud] Mentor texts for narrative writing
required.author: Kate DiCamillo, *The One and Only Ivan* by Katherine Applegate, *Hatchet* by Gary Paulsen, *Sarah, Plain and Tall* by Patricia MacLachlan. These are read for enjoyment and also studied as models of good narrative.

competence_markers.description: The student can freewrite for at least ten minutes without stopping, producing increasingly coherent and idea-rich text
The student can write a personal narrative of six to ten sentences with a clear beginning, middle, and end
The student uses sensory detail in their writing — at least one instance of showing rather than telling in a narrative piece
The student has completed at least three revision cycles (first draft to improved version) with evidence of meaningful change, not just corrected spelling
The student reads their own writing aloud as a revision practice and can identify places that need improvement
The student maintains a Revision Folder with paired first drafts and revised versions showing growth
The student writes daily with increasing volume — paragraph-length entries are the norm, not the exception
The student can articulate what they changed in a revision and why the revised version is better

narration_prompts.detail: How does it work? What happens when you keep writing even when you feel stuck?

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me about a piece of writing you revised.
narration_prompts.detail: What did the first draft look like? What did you change? How is the revised version better?

narration_prompts.prompt: What is the difference between telling the reader something and showing it?
narration_prompts.detail: Give me an example of each.

narration_prompts.prompt: What happens when you read your writing out loud?
narration_prompts.detail: Why is it different from reading silently?

narration_prompts.prompt: Pick a piece from your Revision Folder.
narration_prompts.detail: Read me the first version and the final version. Explain what you changed and why.

narration_prompts.prompt: What is the hardest part of writing for you right now?

cross_connections.description: The narrative fluency developed here feeds directly into the story-writing skills of 3.2.3. Personal narrative — writing about real events with structure and detail — is the foundation on which fictional narrative is built. A student who can write a vivid account of a real moment can learn to write a vivid account of an imagined one.

The revision skills learned here apply directly to expository writing. The student who can look at a narrative and ask "does this make sense? is it in the right order? is there enough detail?" will ask the same questions of an explanation or an argument.

Written narrations of history content are a primary application of the skills in this packet. The student who can write a clear narrative about a real event can write a clear narration of what they learned about ancient Egypt or Mesopotamia.

As the student's writing volume grows, the grammar concepts from 3.1.2 become visible in context. The student notices sentence patterns in their own writing and begins applying grammatical knowledge to revision.

Revision requires accepting that your first draft is not perfect — and that this is normal, not a failure. The resilience developed in IC3.1 supports the emotional capacity to receive feedback and improve, rather than crumble.

Everything in A2 prepares the student for the explicit, structured writing process of Weathering Storms. The student who arrives at B with a daily writing habit, revision experience, and narrative fluency is ready for the formal stages of prewriting, drafting, revising, and editing.

content.why_this_matters:
You already know how to write. You have been putting words in your notebook, telling stories, copying sentences from books you love. Now you are going to learn something that separates real writers from people who just put words on a page: the discipline of writing freely, the craft of telling a complete story, and the power of revision.

Freewriting means writing without stopping — letting words pour out of your pencil the way water pours out of a pitcher. You do not pause to fix spelling. You do not stop to wonder if the sentence is good. You just write. This practice builds a connection between your brain and your hand that nothing else can build. It teaches you that you have more to say than you think you do. Almost every student who tries freewriting for the first time discovers thoughts they did not know they had, because the act of writing pulled those thoughts out of hiding.

Narrative fluency means being able to tell a story — a real, complete story with a beginning, a middle, and an end — smoothly and naturally in writing. You already do this when you talk. Now you will learn to do it on paper with the same ease. When someone asks you to write about something that happened, you will not freeze. You will write the way you would tell the story to a friend, and then you will make it better.

And revision — the word means "to see again." When you revise, you look at what you wrote with fresh eyes and make it stronger, clearer, more interesting. This is where writing actually happens. The first draft is the clay. Revision is the sculpting.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **Freewriting as a daily practice** — the ability to write continuously for a set period without stopping or self-editing
2. **Narrative fluency** — the ability to write a complete, coherent story about a real event with a clear beginning, middle, and end
3. **Revision skills** — the ability to read one's own writing, identify what can be improved, and produce a stronger version
4. **Reading aloud as a revision tool** — using the ear to catch what the eye misses
5. **Expanded daily writing volume** — from sentences to paragraphs, from fragments to complete pieces
6. **The writer's identity** — the student sees themselves as a writer, not as a student completing assignments
content.core_content:
### Part 1: Freewriting — The Pencil Does Not Stop

Here is a new practice. Set a timer for five minutes. Pick up your pencil. Start writing. And do not stop until the timer goes off.

Write about anything. What you ate for breakfast. What the dog did this morning. A dream you had. A story you are making up. It does not matter what. What matters is that you keep writing. If you run out of things to say, write "I don't know what to write" and keep going. Write the same word over and over if you have to. But do not lift your pencil.

This sounds strange. Why would you write nonsense? Because something remarkable happens when you force yourself to keep going: after the "I don't know what to write" phase, your brain starts producing real thoughts. Ideas you did not plan appear on the page. Memories surface. Stories begin to form. The act of writing creates the material for writing. You cannot discover this by thinking about writing. You can only discover it by writing.

Start with five minutes. When five minutes feels easy, move to eight. Then ten. Then fifteen. By the end of this packet, you should be able to freewrite for fifteen minutes without stopping. That is roughly two handwritten pages — and you will be astonished by what shows up on those pages.

**The rules of freewriting:**
1. The pencil does not stop moving
2. Do not erase anything
3. Do not worry about spelling or punctuation
4. Do not go back and fix things
5. If you get stuck, write about being stuck
6. When the timer goes off, stop — and read what you wrote

### Part 2: Writing About Real Things — Narrative Fluency

A narrative is a story about something that really happened. You are already a natural narrator — when you come home and say, "You won't believe what happened at the park today!" you are narrating. Now you are going to bring that same energy to the page.

Here is the structure of a good personal narrative:

**The beginning** hooks the reader. It puts them in a moment. Not "One day I went to the park" (boring — nothing has happened yet), but "The first thing I saw when we got to the park was the dog standing on top of the picnic table" (now the reader is curious — why is there a dog on the table?).

**The middle** tells what happened, in order, with details that make the reader feel like they were there. Not "The dog was funny" (that tells the reader what to think instead of letting them experience it), but "The dog had a hot dog bun in its mouth and was wagging its tail so hard its whole body was wiggling" (now the reader can see it and decide for themselves that it is funny).

**The end** lands the story. It does not just stop — it finishes. Something is resolved, or something is understood, or something has changed. "We laughed all the way home, and every time we pass that park now, my brother says, 'Check the tables.'" Now the story feels complete.

Practice writing narratives about real events from your life. Start with small moments — not "my vacation" (too big) but "the moment the wave knocked me over" (small enough to write with detail). Small moments make the best stories because you can include the specific details that make writing come alive.

### Part 3: Sensory Detail — Making the Reader Feel It

Good writers do not just tell you what happened. They make you experience it. They do this with sensory detail — details that appeal to the five senses.

- **Sight:** "The kitchen was a mess" versus "Flour covered the counter and the floor and somehow the dog's back"
- **Sound:** "It was noisy" versus "The screen door slammed every thirty seconds as kids ran in and out"
- **Smell:** "It smelled good" versus "The whole house smelled like cinnamon and browning butter"
- **Touch:** "It was cold" versus "The metal railing burned my hand with cold, and I let go fast"
- **Taste:** "The soup was bad" versus "The soup tasted like hot water with a rumor of chicken"

When you revise your writing, look for places where you told the reader what to think ("it was scary") and replace them with details that let the reader experience it ("my stomach felt like it dropped to the floor, and I could hear my own breathing"). This is one of the most powerful revision moves you can learn.

### Part 4: Revision — Seeing Your Writing Again

You wrote a draft. Good. Now comes the real work.

Revision is not fixing spelling mistakes. That is editing — and it comes last. Revision is looking at your writing and asking bigger questions:

**Does this make sense?** Read your piece from the beginning as if you had never seen it before. Is there a part where a reader would be confused? Where you skipped something important? Where you explained something you did not need to explain?

**Is it in the right order?** Sometimes you realize that the story should start at a different point, or that a paragraph needs to move. You can cut sentences apart and rearrange them, or simply rewrite the piece in a new order.

**Is there enough detail?** Find the most important moment in your piece. Is it the most detailed part? It should be. The moment that matters most needs the most specific writing. If you spent three sentences on the boring walk to the park and one sentence on the amazing thing that happened there, your proportions are wrong.

**Are there words or sentences that are not doing any work?** Every sentence should earn its place. If you can remove a sentence and the piece is just as good without it, remove it. This is hard — especially when you like the sentence. But good writing is not about keeping everything you wrote. It is about keeping only what the piece needs.

### Part 5: Reading Aloud — Your Best Revision Tool

Here is the most powerful revision technique in the world: read your writing out loud.

When you read silently, your brain fixes errors automatically — you read what you meant to write, not what you actually wrote. When you read aloud, your ear catches everything: the sentence that goes on too long, the word that is repeated three times in a row, the place where the rhythm stumbles, the spot where you forgot a word. Your ear is a better editor than your eye.

Practice this: after every piece of writing you want to improve, read it aloud to yourself or to someone else. Mark the places where you stumble or where something does not sound right. Then fix those places. This single practice will improve your writing more than any other technique.

### Part 6: The Revision Folder — Watching Yourself Grow

Not everything you write needs to be revised. Your daily notebook stays as it is — raw, free, unpolished. But several times during this packet, you will choose a piece of writing to revise seriously. When you do, put both versions in your Revision Folder: the first draft and the revised version, side by side.

Over time, this folder becomes proof that you are growing as a writer. You can look back at early drafts and see how far you have come. You can show someone your first version and your final version and explain what you changed and why. This is not just a record — it is a source of pride. The Revision Folder shows that you are not just writing. You are working at writing. That is what real writers do.
content.formative_practices:
### Study 1: Daily Freewriting [Ongoing — Backbone of the Packet]
The student freewrites every day, starting at five minutes and building to fifteen. The mentor sits nearby and also freewrites. After the timer, both share one sentence or idea they discovered. The focus is on building the habit and the neural pathway, not on producing polished work.

### Study 2: Personal Narrative Writing [3-4 Complete Narratives During the Packet]
The student writes personal narratives about specific small moments. The mentor helps the student choose moments that are specific enough to write with detail: "the time the power went out" rather than "my summer." Each narrative goes through at least one round of revision.

### Study 3: Sensory Detail Practice [Ongoing]
During read-alouds, the mentor points out sensory details: "Listen to how the author describes the kitchen. She doesn't say 'it was warm.' She says the stove made the air heavy and sweet." The student practices adding sensory detail to their own writing, starting with a single revision move: find one place where you told the reader what to feel, and replace it with a detail that shows it.

### Study 4: Revision Workshop [At Least 3 Times During the Packet]
The student selects a piece from their notebook, reads it aloud, identifies what to improve, and produces a revised version. The mentor guides with questions, not instructions: "What is the most important part of this story? Does the reader get enough detail there? Is there a part that feels slow or unnecessary?" The student makes the decisions.

### Study 5: Mentor Text Study [Monthly]
The mentor and student examine a short passage from a well-written book. Together they notice: How does the author begin? How does the author use detail? How does the author end? The student then tries a similar technique in their own writing. This is not imitation — it is learning by studying craft.

### Study 6: The Revision Folder Review [End of Packet]
The student reviews their Revision Folder — all the first drafts and revised versions collected during the packet. Together with the mentor, they identify: What are you doing better now than at the start? What revision moves do you make most often? What do you still want to improve? This review builds metacognition — the ability to think about your own writing process.

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Freewriting Capacity
**Developing well:** The student writes continuously for the allotted time. After the initial "I don't know what to write" phase (which has passed), the freewriting contains genuine observations, story fragments, questions, and ideas. The student sometimes finds material in freewriting that they want to develop into a full piece.

**Developing slowly:** The student still stops frequently during freewriting. The output is minimal or repetitive. The student has not experienced the breakthrough of writing through resistance.

**Diagnostic questions:** Are you freewriting alongside the student? (Modeling matters enormously.) Is the environment quiet and focused? Is the student being asked to share their raw freewriting? (This can create performance anxiety — share only discoveries, not full texts.)

### 2. Narrative Quality
**Developing well:** The student's personal narratives have identifiable beginnings, middles, and ends. They include specific details — names, sensory impressions, dialogue. The reader can follow what happened and why it mattered. The narratives are getting longer and more detailed over the course of the packet.

**Developing slowly:** Narratives are lists of events without structure or detail. "We went to the store. We bought stuff. We came home." No sense of what mattered or why the story is worth telling.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is the student choosing moments that are small enough to write with detail? (The "tell me about your summer" prompt produces terrible writing from everyone. "Tell me about the moment you stepped off the boat" produces vivid writing.) Are you having the pre-writing conversation?

### 3. Revision Engagement
**Developing well:** The student engages with revision willingly. They can read their work aloud, identify at least one thing to improve, and produce a meaningfully different revised version. They understand that the revised version is better and can explain why.

**Developing slowly:** The student resists revision. They view any suggestion to change their writing as criticism. The "revised" version is identical to the first draft except for corrected spelling.

**Diagnostic questions:** Have you modeled revision with your own writing? Has the student seen you cut a sentence you liked because it did not serve the piece? Are you framing revision as power (you get to make it better) rather than as correction (it is not good enough)?

### 4. Writing Identity
**Developing well:** The student refers to themselves as a writer. They sometimes choose to write outside of scheduled time. They have opinions about their own writing — pieces they are proud of, techniques they want to try. They notice craft in the books they read: "I like how that author started the chapter."

**Developing slowly:** The student still views writing as something done for an assignment or an adult's approval. They do not choose to write voluntarily. They have no opinions about their own writing.

**Diagnostic questions:** Does the student have genuine choice in what they write about? Is the notebook truly theirs? Do you talk about writing with enthusiasm, or as a chore to get through?
mentor_guide.common_pitfalls:
### Turning Freewriting into an Assignment
Freewriting is not a writing prompt. Do not say "Today, freewrite about your grandmother." That defeats the purpose. The student chooses what to write. The only constraint is the timer and the rule that the pencil does not stop. If you direct the content, you have turned a generative exercise into an assignment.

### Revising Too Many Pieces
Not everything the student writes should be revised. The daily notebook stays raw. Freewriting stays raw. Only a few pieces per month are selected for revision — pieces the student cares about enough to invest the effort. If every piece of writing must be revised, revision becomes drudgery instead of a craft practice.

### Prioritizing Grammar Correction as Revision
A student who "revises" by fixing spelling and adding capital letters has not revised — they have edited. Revision is structural: adding detail where it is needed, cutting what is unnecessary, reorganizing for clarity, strengthening the opening, landing the ending. Editing is important, but it comes after revision, not instead of it. If the student's "revisions" are all surface-level, you need to redirect with structural questions.

### Insufficient Read-Alouds
The read-aloud continues through A2 and should not be dropped. The student's writing will mirror the quality of the language they hear. A student who hears beautiful prose daily will unconsciously adopt its rhythms. A student who hears only instructions and assignments will write like an instruction manual.

### Comparing Students to Each Other
If you mentor multiple students, resist all comparison. Writing development is wildly variable. A seven-year-old who produces two sentences may outwrite the ten-year-old who produces ten sentences within a year, because development is nonlinear. Evaluate each student against their own past performance, not against anyone else's.
mentor_guide.narration_evaluation:
### Prompt 1: What is Freewriting?
**Strong:** Explains the practice clearly — writing without stopping, not worrying about mistakes, discovering ideas by writing through resistance. May describe a personal experience of finding an unexpected idea through freewriting.
**Weak:** "It is when you write fast." No understanding of the purpose or the experience.

### Prompt 2: A Revision Experience
**Strong:** Describes specific changes — "I added more detail about what the room looked like" or "I cut the first two sentences because the story actually starts in the third sentence." Can explain why the revision improved the piece.
**Weak:** "I fixed the spelling." No structural revision described.

### Prompt 3: Showing versus Telling
**Strong:** Can explain the concept and give an original example. "Instead of saying 'I was happy,' I could say 'I couldn't stop smiling and I kept bouncing on my toes.'"
**Weak:** Cannot explain the concept or gives only the examples from the packet without generating their own.

### Prompt 5: Revision Folder Comparison
**Strong:** Can read both versions, identify what changed, and explain why the changes made the piece better. Shows pride in the improvement.
**Weak:** Cannot articulate what changed or why it matters. The two versions are nearly identical.
